title.zh: 卷四十上 班彪列傳
title.raw: 卷四十上 班彪列傳 Volume 40a: Biography of Ban Biao 1

sentences.zh: 後漢書卷四十上 班彪列傳 第三十上

translations.literal: Book of Later Han, Scroll 40 Part One: Biography of Ban Biao, Number Thirty Part One.
translations.idiomatic: Book of Later Han, scroll 40a: Biography of Ban Biao, part one.

sentences.zh: 自東都主人以下分為下卷*

translations.literal: From "The Host of the Eastern Capital" onward is divided into the lower scroll.*
translations.idiomatic: Material from "The Host of the Eastern Capital" onward appears in the following scroll.*

sentences.zh: 班彪字叔皮，扶風安陵人也。

translations.literal: Ban Biao, courtesy name Shupi, was a man of Anling in Fufeng.
translations.idiomatic: Ban Biao, courtesy name Shupi, came from Anling in Fufeng commandery.

sentences.zh: 祖況，成帝時為越騎校尉。

translations.literal: His grandfather Kuang, in the time of Emperor Cheng, served as colonel of the agile cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Ban Kuang held the post of colonel of the agile cavalry under Emperor Cheng.

sentences.zh: 父稚，哀帝時為廣平太守。

translations.literal: His father Zhi, in the time of Emperor Ai, was governor of Guangping.
translations.idiomatic: His father Ban Zhi served as governor of Guangping under Emperor Ai.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]廣平，郡，今洺州永*

translations.literal: [One] Commentary [one]: Guangping was a commandery; present-day Yongnian in Mingzhou.*
translations.idiomatic: Note: Guangping was a commandery; its seat corresponds to Yongnian in present-day Mingzhou.*

translations.literal: (ping)
translations.idiomatic: (The character "ping," completing the place-name.)

sentences.zh: **[年]*縣也，隋室諱廣改焉。

translations.literal: **[year]* was a county; the Sui court avoided the taboo on "Guang" and changed the name.
translations.idiomatic: It was a county; the Sui dynasty renamed it to avoid the taboo on the character guang.

sentences.zh: 彪性沉重好古。

translations.literal: Ban Biao by nature was grave and fond of antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: Ban Biao was sober-minded by temperament and drawn to classical learning.

sentences.zh: 年二十餘，更始敗，三輔大亂。

translations.literal: When he was over twenty years old, Gengshi fell; the Three Adjuncts were greatly thrown into chaos.
translations.idiomatic: In his twenties he lived through the collapse of the Gengshi regime and the turmoil that swept the capital region.

sentences.zh: 時隗囂擁觿天水，彪乃避難從之。

translations.literal: At that time Wei Xiao occupied Tianshui; Ban Biao then fled calamity and joined him.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiao had seized control of Tianshui, so Ban Biao took refuge and threw in his lot with him.

sentences.zh: 囂問彪曰：「往者周亡，戰國並爭，天下分裂，數世然後定。

translations.literal: Wei Xiao asked Ban Biao, saying: "In former times when Zhou perished, the Warring States contended together; the realm split apart and only after several generations was it settled.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiao asked him: "When the Zhou fell and the Warring States tore one another apart, the empire shattered and generations passed before order returned.

sentences.zh: 意者從橫之事復起於今乎？

translations.literal: Does one suppose that affairs of alliance and defiance will arise again in our day?
translations.idiomatic: Do you think we are heading into another age of rival alliances and betrayals?

sentences.zh: 將承運迭興，在於一人也？

translations.literal: Or will those who receive the mandate rise in succession, resting in one man?
translations.idiomatic: Or will Heaven raise successive heirs to the mandate under a single ruler?

sentences.zh: 願生試論之。

translations.literal: I wish you would try to discuss it.
translations.idiomatic: I should like to hear your view.

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「周之廢興，與漢殊異。

translations.literal: " He replied, saying: "The decline and rise of Zhou differ greatly from Han.
translations.idiomatic: Ban Biao replied: "The fall and revival of Zhou bear little resemblance to our Han situation.

sentences.zh: 昔周爵五等，諸侯從政，本根既微，枝葉強大，故其末流有從橫之事，埶數然也。

translations.literal: Formerly Zhou ranked nobility in five grades; the feudal lords participated in government; the root was already slight while branches and leaves grew mighty; therefore in its latter flow there were alliance-and-defiance affairs—the tendency of the configuration was thus.
translations.idiomatic: Under Zhou the feudal order ran five ranks deep: barons shared power at court while the royal trunk grew weak and the regional houses grew strong, which is why the age ended in shifting leagues and betrayals—the logic of that political geometry.

sentences.zh: 漢承秦制，改立郡縣，主有專己之威，臣無百年之柄。

translations.literal: Han inherited Qin's system, altered it and established commanderies and counties; the ruler possesses awe exclusive to himself; ministers have no hundred-year grip on power.
translations.idiomatic: Han took over Qin's bureaucratic order of commanderies and counties: the sovereign holds concentrated authority, and no minister can cling to office across generations.

sentences.zh: 至於成帝，假借外家，[一]哀、平短祚，國嗣三絕，[二]故王氏□朝，因竊號位。

translations.literal: As for Emperor Cheng, he lent authority to his maternal kin; [one] Emperors Ai and Ping had short reigns; the imperial succession was cut off three times; [two] therefore the Wang clan faced the court and seized titles and position.
translations.idiomatic: Under Cheng the throne leaned on the Wang in-laws; Ai and Ping ruled briefly, and the succession failed three times over, so the Wangs moved into the palace and usurped the markers of sovereignty.

sentences.zh: 危自上起，傷不及下，[三]是以即真之後，天下莫不引領而歎。

translations.literal: The peril arose from above; harm did not reach below; [three] therefore after he assumed true rule, none in the realm did not crane their necks and sigh.
translations.idiomatic: The danger came from the court, not from the common people, [three] which is why, once Guangwu took the throne, everyone looked up with longing.

sentences.zh: 十餘年閒，中外搔擾，遠近俱發，假號雲合，咸稱劉氏，不謀同辭。

translations.literal: Within more than ten years, inner and outer were disturbed; far and near alike rose; false titles gathered like clouds—without plotting they spoke with one voice, all styling themselves Liu.
translations.idiomatic: For more than a decade the heartland and the marches were in uproar; rebels sprouted everywhere, borrowed banners clustered like clouds, and claimant after claimant declared himself a Liu—yet no conspiracy coordinated their slogans.

sentences.zh: [四]方今雄桀帶州域者，皆無七國世業之資，而百姓謳吟，思仰漢德，已可知矣。

translations.literal: [Four] Now those heroes who gird whole provinces possess none of the hereditary resources of the Seven States, yet the hundred surnames chant songs and yearn toward Han virtue—this can already be known.
translations.idiomatic: [Four] Today's warlords who dominate whole provinces lack the entrenched dynastic foundations the Warring States barons enjoyed, while ordinary folk sing their loyalty—everyone can see where hearts still lie.

sentences.zh: 」囂曰：「生言周、漢之埶可也；

translations.literal: " Wei Xiao said: "What you say about the situations of Zhou and Han may stand;
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xiao replied: "Your contrast between Zhou and Han rings true.

sentences.zh: 至於但見愚人習識劉氏姓號之故，而謂漢家復興，□矣。

translations.literal: As for merely seeing foolish men accustomed to recognize the surname and style-name of Liu and therefore saying the House of Han revives—that is mistaken.
translations.idiomatic: But to hear rustics chant the old Liu name and conclude that Han must rise again is badly mistaken.

sentences.zh: 昔秦失其鹿，劉季逐而羈之，時人復知漢乎？

translations.literal: Formerly when Qin lost its deer, Liu Ji chased and tethered it—did people of the time again know of Han?
translations.idiomatic: When Qin dropped the prize of empire, Liu Bang seized it—did anyone then foresee the Han dynasty?

translations.literal: Closing quotation mark; commentary marker five.
translations.idiomatic: Close of Wei Xiao's quoted speech; superscript commentary note five.

sentences.zh: 注[一]外家謂王鳳、王商等，並輔政領尚書事也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: By maternal kin are meant Wang Feng, Wang Shang, and such—all assisting governance and directing Secretariat affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The Wang uncles—Wang Feng, Wang Shang, and their ilk—held the reins of government and ran the Secretariat.

sentences.zh: 注[二]哀帝在位六年，平帝在位五年，故曰短祚。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Emperor Ai was on the throne six years; Emperor Ping five years—therefore it is called short reign.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Ai ruled six years and Ping five, hence "short reigns."

sentences.zh: 成、哀、平俱無子，是三絕也。

translations.literal: Cheng, Ai, and Ping all lacked sons—this is the threefold cutting off.
translations.idiomatic: None of the three—Cheng, Ai, or Ping—left an heir; that is the "three breaks" in the succession.

sentences.zh: 注[三]成帝威權借於外家，是危自上起也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Emperor Cheng's awesome authority was lent to maternal kin—this is peril arising from above.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Cheng delegated real power to his in-laws, so the crisis began at court.

sentences.zh: 漢德無害於百姓，是傷不及下也。

translations.literal: Han virtue did no harm to the hundred surnames—this is injury not reaching below.
translations.idiomatic: Han had done the common people no wrong, so the suffering did not rise from the countryside.

sentences.zh: 注[四]謂王郎、盧芳等並詐稱劉氏也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Refers to Wang Lang, Lu Fang, and others all falsely claiming to be Liu.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Rebels such as Wang Lang and Lu Fang falsely claimed Liu descent.

sentences.zh: 注[五]太公六韜曰：「取天下如逐鹿，鹿得，天下共分其肉也。」

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: The Grand Duke's Six Secret Teachings says: "Taking the realm is like chasing a deer; when the deer is obtained, the realm shares its meat together."
translations.idiomatic: Note: The Six Secret Teachings ascribed to Taigong compares winning the empire to hunting a deer—once it falls, everyone claims a slice.

sentences.zh: 彪既疾囂言，又傷時方艱，乃著王命論，以為漢德承堯，有靈命之符，王者興祚，非詐力所致，欲以感之，而囂終不寤，遂避河西。

translations.literal: Ban Biao both resented Wei Xiao's words and grieved that the times were hard; he therefore composed the Discourse on the King's Mandate, holding that Han virtue succeeded Yao and possessed tokens of numinous mandate—that rise of the king and blessing of fate are not brought about by deceit and force—wishing thereby to move him, but Wei Xiao in the end did not awaken; he then fled to Hexi.
translations.idiomatic: Angered by Wei Xiao's cynicism and distressed by the strife around him, Ban Biao wrote his Discourse on the King's Mandate, arguing that Han inherited Yao's legitimacy and bore Heaven's signs—that true kingship rests on mandate, not fraud—and hoping to sway Wei Xiao. When Xiao would not listen, Ban crossed into Hexi.

sentences.zh: 河西大將軍竇融以為從事，深敬待之，接以師友之道。

translations.literal: The Grand General of Hexi, Dou Rong, employed him as an aide and deeply respected him, receiving him with the way of teacher and friend.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Rong, supreme commander west of the Yellow River, appointed him adjutant, honored him deeply, and treated him as both mentor and companion.

sentences.zh: 彪乃為融畫策事漢，總西河以拒隗囂。

translations.literal: Ban Biao then for Dou Rong laid plans to serve Han, gathering the western river region to resist Wei Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: Ban drafted the strategy by which Dou pledged loyalty to Han and rallied the northwest against Wei Xiao.

sentences.zh: 及融征還京師，光武問曰：「所上章奏，誰與參之？

translations.literal: When Dou Rong was summoned back to the capital, Guangwu asked, saying: "The memorials you submitted—with whom did you consult in drafting them?
translations.idiomatic: When Dou Rong was recalled to Luoyang, Guangwu asked him who had helped draft his memorials.

sentences.zh: 」融對曰：「皆從事班彪所為。

translations.literal: " Dou Rong replied, saying: "They were all done by aide Ban Biao.
translations.idiomatic: Dou answered, "My adjutant Ban Biao wrote every one of them."

sentences.zh: 」帝雅聞彪才，因召入見，舉司隸茂才，拜徐令，以病免。

translations.literal: " The emperor had long heard of Ban Biao's talent; he therefore summoned him to audience, nominated him as outstanding talent through the metropolitan governor, appointed him magistrate of Xu, and dismissed him on grounds of illness.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor had heard of Ban's abilities and summoned him, nominated him as recommended talent through the metropolitan governor, named him magistrate of Xu, then accepted his resignation on grounds of ill health.

sentences.zh: [一]後數應三公之命，輒去。

translations.literal: [One] Later he repeatedly responded to summons from the Three Excellencies but always departed.
translations.idiomatic: [One] He was repeatedly summoned by the Three Excellencies but each time declined the appointment.

sentences.zh: 注[一]司隸舉為茂才也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: It means nominated as outstanding talent by the metropolitan governor.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The metropolitan governor had recommended him as an outstanding talent.

sentences.zh: 徐，縣，屬臨淮郡。

translations.literal: Xu was a county belonging to Linhuai commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Xu county lay in Linhuai commandery.

sentences.zh: 彪既才高而好述作，遂專心史籍之閒。

translations.literal: Ban Biao being both talented and fond of composition, he thereupon devoted himself among historical records.
translations.idiomatic: Gifted and devoted to writing, Ban threw himself into historical scholarship.

sentences.zh: 武帝時，司馬遷著史記，自太初以後，闕而不錄，[一]後好事者頗或綴集時事，然多鄙俗，不足以踵繼其書。

translations.literal: In Emperor Wu's time Sima Qian composed the Records of the Grand Historian; from Taichu onward it was lacking and not recorded; [one] later persons fond of such matters somewhat stitched together current affairs, yet mostly vulgar and insufficient to follow and continue his book.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wu, Sima Qian wrote the Records of the Grand Historian, but he stopped with the Taichu era; [one] later enthusiasts patched together continuations, yet most were crude and unworthy sequels.

sentences.zh: [二]彪乃繼采前史遺事，傍貫異聞，作後傳數十篇，因斟酌前史而譏正得失。

translations.literal: [Two] Ban Biao then continued gathering leftover matters from former histories, threading through strange hearsay, composing several tens of pieces of Later Traditions, thereby weighing former histories and criticizing and correcting gain and loss.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] Ban gathered what earlier histories had left unsaid, worked in corroborating anecdotes, and wrote several dozen chapters of Later Traditions, reassessing prior accounts and weighing their strengths and faults.

sentences.zh: 其略論曰： 注[一]太初，武帝年號。

translations.literal: His summary discourse says: Commentary [one]: Taichu was Emperor Wu's reign title.
translations.idiomatic: His synopsis opens thus: Note: Taichu was Emperor Wu's reign title.

sentences.zh: 注[二]好事者謂楊雄、劉歆、陽城衡、褚少孫、史孝山之徒也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Those fond of such matters means Yang Xiong, Liu Xin, Yangcheng Heng, Chu Shaosun, Shi Xiaoshan, and their sort.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The enthusiasts Ban refers to include Yang Xiong, Liu Xin, Yangcheng Heng, Chu Shaosun, Shi Xiaoshan, and others like them.

sentences.zh: 唐虞三代，詩書所及，世有史官，以司典籍，[一]暨於諸侯，國自有史，[二] 故孟子曰「楚之檮杌，晉之乘，魯之春秋，其事一也」。

translations.literal: In the eras of Tang-Yao, Yu-Shun, and the Three Dynasties, as reached by the Classic of Poetry and Documents, each age had scribes to oversee texts; [one] extending to the feudal lords, each state had its own history; [two] therefore Mencius said: "Chu's Taowu, Jin's Sheng, Lu's Spring and Autumn—their affairs are one."
translations.idiomatic: From the legendary sage-kings through the Three Dynasties, the Classics show every court employing chroniclers; [one] among the feudal states each kept its own annals, [two] which is why Mencius observed that Chu called its records Taowu, Jin called theirs Sheng, and Lu called theirs Spring and Autumn—yet they performed the same office.

sentences.zh: [三]定哀之閒，[四]魯君子左丘明論集其文，作左氏傳三十篇，又撰異同，號曰國語，二十一篇，由是乘、檮杌之事遂闇，[五]而左氏、國語獨章。

translations.literal: [Three] Between Dukes Ding and Ai, [four] the gentleman of Lu Zuo Qiuming arranged and gathered its texts, composing the Zuo Tradition in thirty scrolls; again compiling similarities and differences, styled Discourses of the States in twenty-one scrolls—thereby the affairs of Sheng and Taowu grew dark; [five] while Zuo's tradition and Discourses of the States alone shone forth.
translations.idiomatic: [Three] During the reigns of Dukes Ding and Ai of Lu, [four] Zuo Qiuming compiled Lu's archives into thirty chapters of what we call the Zuo Commentary and assembled twenty-one chapters of parallel traditions as the Discourses of the States; [five] older state chronicles such as Jin's Sheng and Chu's Taowu then faded while Zuo's works carried the field.

sentences.zh: 又有記錄黃帝以來至春秋時帝王公侯卿大夫，號曰世本，一十五篇。

translations.literal: Again there was one recording from the Yellow Emperor down to Spring and Autumn times emperors, kings, dukes, marquises, ministers, and grand officers, styled Genealogical Origins, fifteen scrolls.
translations.idiomatic: Another compilation traced imperial and noble lines from the Yellow Emperor through the Spring and Autumn period under the title Genealogical Origins in fifteen scrolls.

sentences.zh: 春秋之後，七國並爭，秦並諸侯，則有戰國策三十三篇。

translations.literal: After Spring and Autumn, the Seven States contended together; Qin annexed the feudal lords—thereupon there were Intrigues of the Warring States in thirty-three scrolls.
translations.idiomatic: After the Spring and Autumn era the Seven States fought for supremacy until Qin swallowed them; their stratagems were preserved in thirty-three scrolls of Intrigues of the Warring States.

sentences.zh: 漢興定天下，太中大夫陸賈記錄時功，作楚漢春秋九篇。

translations.literal: When Han arose and settled the realm, Grand Counsellor of the Palace Lu Jia recorded timely achievements, composing Spring and Autumn of Chu and Han in nine scrolls.
translations.idiomatic: After Han's founding, Palace Counsellor Lu Jia chronicled the rise of Liu Bang and Xiang Yu in nine chapters titled Spring and Autumn of Chu and Han.

sentences.zh: 孝武之世，太史令司馬遷采左氏、國語，刪世本、戰國策，據楚、漢列國時事，上自黃帝，下訖獲麟，[六]作本紀、世家、列傳、書、表凡百三十篇，而十篇缺焉。

translations.literal: In Emperor Xiaowu's age, Grand Astrologer Sima Qian drew from Zuo's tradition and Discourses of the States, excised Genealogical Origins and Intrigues of the Warring States, relied on Chu-Han and feudal states' current affairs, above from the Yellow Emperor, below ending at capturing the unicorn, [six] composing Basic Annals, Hereditary Houses, Arrayed Traditions, Treatises, and Tables—altogether one hundred thirty scrolls—yet ten scrolls are missing.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wu, Sima Qian as Grand Astrologer reworked Zuo, Discourses of the States, Genealogical Origins, and Intrigues of the Warring States into a narrative running from the Yellow Emperor down to the unicorn portent of 122 BC, [six] organizing it as annals, hereditary houses, biographies, treatises, and tables—130 chapters in all, ten of them later lost.

sentences.zh: [七]遷之所記，從漢元至武以絕，則其功也。

translations.literal: [Seven] What Qian recorded, following from Han's inception to Wu then stopping—that is his achievement.
translations.idiomatic: [Seven] His lasting achievement was to carry Han history from its beginnings down through Emperor Wu.

sentences.zh: 至於采經摭傳，分散百家之事，甚多□略，不如其本，務欲以多聞廣載為功，論議淺而不篤。

translations.literal: As for gathering classics and plucking commentaries, scattering the matters of the hundred schools—very many lacunae and omissions, not matching their roots; striving to take broad hearing and wide recording as achievement—discussion shallow and not solid.
translations.idiomatic: Yet when he stitched together classics and commentaries and tossed in anecdotes from every school, he often blurred sources and privileged sheer bulk over coherence; his judgments stay shallow.

sentences.zh: 其論術學，則崇黃老而薄五經；

translations.literal: In his discussing arts and learning, then he esteems Huang-Lao and belittles the Five Classics;
translations.idiomatic: On scholarship he favors Daoist Huang-Lao lore and slight Confucian scripture;

sentences.zh: [八]序貨殖，則輕仁義而羞貧窮；

translations.literal: [Eight] In ordering treatises on commodity exchange, then he slightens benevolence and righteousness and shames poverty;
translations.idiomatic: [Eight] in his chapter on merchants he treats morality as secondary and poverty as shameful;

sentences.zh: [九]道遊俠，則賤守節而貴俗功：[一０]此其大敝傷道，所以遇極刑之咎也。

translations.literal: [Nine] In treating roaming bravoes, then he debases guarding integrity and esteems vulgar achievement: [ten] these are his great flaws harming the Way—therefore he met the blame of extreme punishment.
translations.idiomatic: [nine] in portraying wandering swordsmen he celebrates worldly swagger over steadfast virtue—[ten] grave moral blind spots that foreshadowed his brutal sentence.

sentences.zh: [一一]然善述序事理，辯而不華，質而不野，文質相稱，蓋良史之才也。

translations.literal: [Eleven] Yet he is good at narrating and ordering affairs and principles—discriminating yet not ornate, plain yet not rustic; pattern and substance matching each other—this is surely the talent of a fine historian.
translations.idiomatic: [Eleven] Still, his sequencing of events is lucid and restrained—precise without ornament, plain without crudeness—a balance worthy of a master historian.

sentences.zh: 誠令遷依五經之法言，同聖人之是非，意亦庶幾矣。

translations.literal: If truly one made Qian follow the normative words of the Five Classics and align with the sage's judgments of right and wrong—his intent would also be nearly there.
translations.idiomatic: Had Sima Qian grounded every judgment in the Classics and the sage's moral scale, he would have come close to perfection.

sentences.zh: [一二] 注[一]禮記曰：「動則左史書之，言則右史書之。

translations.literal: [Twelve] Commentary [one]: The Record of Rites says: "When there is movement the left scribe records it; when there is speech the right scribe records it."
translations.idiomatic: [Twelve] Note: The Record of Rites assigns speeches to the right scribe and deeds to the left.

sentences.zh: 」見於史籍者，夏太史終古、殷太史向摯、周太史儋也。

translations.literal: " Those seen in historical records are Xia Grand Scribe Zhonggu, Yin Grand Scribe Xiang Zhi, Zhou Grand Scribe Dan.
translations.idiomatic: Named holders of that office include Xia's Zhonggu, Yin's Xiang Zhi, and Zhou's Dan.

sentences.zh: 見呂氏春秋。

translations.literal: Seen in Master Lü's Spring and Autumn.
translations.idiomatic: See Master Lü's Spring and Autumn.

sentences.zh: 注[二]左傳，魯季孫召外史掌惡臣。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Zuo's Tradition—Lu's Jisun summoned outside scribes to oversee wicked ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Zuo's Commentary mentions Lu's Jisun summoning outside scribes to record wicked officers.

sentences.zh: □史華龍滑「曰我太史」也。

translations.literal: Lu outside scribes Hua Long and Hua Huai said, "We are the grand scribes"—thus.
translations.idiomatic: Archivists Hua Long and Hua Huai answered the summons with "We hold the grand scribe's office" (see Zuo's Commentary).

sentences.zh: 楚有左史倚相。

translations.literal: Chu had left scribe Yi Xiang.
translations.idiomatic: Chu employed the chronicler Yi Xiang.

sentences.zh: 注[三]乘者，興於田賦乘馬之事。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Sheng means arising from field-tax and team-horse affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The title Sheng probably derives from levies of horses and grain.

sentences.zh: 檮杌者，嚚凶之類，興於記惡之誡。

translations.literal: Taowu means stubborn fierce sorts—arising from recording evil as warning.
translations.idiomatic: Taowu denotes vicious conduct recorded as a moral warning.

sentences.zh: 春秋以二始舉四時，以記萬事，遂各因以為名，其記事一也。

translations.literal: Spring and Autumn uses two beginnings to raise the four seasons, thereby recording the myriad affairs—thereupon each accordingly takes it as name; their recording affairs is one.
translations.idiomatic: Spring and Autumn calendars pair two beginnings with the four seasons to encompass every affair; states coined different titles, yet each chronicle served the same purpose.

sentences.zh: 見趙岐孟子注。

translations.literal: Seen in Zhao Qi's commentary on Mencius.
translations.idiomatic: See Zhao Qi's subcommentary on Mencius.

sentences.zh: 注[四]魯定公、哀公也注[五]不行於時為闇也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Lu's Dukes Ding and Ai also commentary [five]: not practiced in the times is dark.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The reigns of Dukes Ding and Ai of Lu; [five] texts that fell out of circulation are called "dark."

sentences.zh: 其書今亡。

translations.literal: Its book is now lost.
translations.idiomatic: Those works are no longer extant.

sentences.zh: 注[六]武帝太始二年，登隴首，獲白麟，遷作史記，絕筆於此年也。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: Emperor Wu's Taishi second year, ascended Longtou, captured white unicorn—Qian composed Records of the Grand Historian, ending the brush in this year.
translations.idiomatic: Note: In Taishi 2 (95 BC) Emperor Wu captured a white unicorn on Longtou; Sima Qian ended his Records at that omen.

sentences.zh: 注[七]十篇謂遷歿之後，亡景紀、武紀、禮書、樂書、兵書、將相年表、日者傳、三王世家、龜策傳、傅靳列傳。

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: The ten scrolls mean after Qian's death were lost Basic Annals of Jing, Basic Annals of Wu, Treatise on Rites, Treatise on Music, Treatise on Military Affairs, Chronological Table of Generals and Ministers, Tradition of Diviners, Hereditary House of Three Kings, Tradition of Tortoise and Milfoil, Traditions of Fu and Jin.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The ten missing chapters include Jing's and Wu's annals, several treatises, one table, and several biographical chapters listed here.

sentences.zh: 注[八]黃帝、老子，道家也。

translations.literal: Commentary [eight]: Yellow Emperor and Laozi—Daoist school.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Huang-Lao denotes the Daoist tradition.

sentences.zh: 五經，儒家也。

translations.literal: The Five Classics—Confucian school.
translations.idiomatic: The Five Classics belong to Confucian teaching.

sentences.zh: 遷序傳曰：「道家使人精神專一，動合無形，贍足萬物。

translations.literal: Qian's authorial tradition says: "The Daoist school makes people's spirit concentrated and one; movement matches the formless; sufficient for the myriad things."
translations.idiomatic: In his summary chapter Sima Qian writes that Daoism "focuses the spirit, moves with the formless, and nourishes the ten thousand things."

sentences.zh: 」此謂崇黃老也。

translations.literal: " This is called esteeming Huang-Lao.
translations.idiomatic: That passage illustrates his esteem for Huang-Lao.

sentences.zh: 又曰：「儒者博而寡要，勞而少功。

translations.literal: Again it says: "Confucians are broad yet grasp few essentials; toil yet little achievement."
translations.idiomatic: Elsewhere he complains that Confucians "know a little about everything yet miss the essentials; they labor yet accomplish little."

sentences.zh: 」此為薄五經也。

translations.literal: " This is belittling the Five Classics.
translations.idiomatic: That is how he slighted the canonical texts.

sentences.zh: 注[九]史記貨殖傳序曰：「家貧親老，妻子剁弱，歲時無以祭祀，飲食被服不足以自適，如此不籩恥，則無所比矣。

translations.literal: Commentary [nine]: Records of the Grand Historian preface to Traditions of Commodity Exchange says: "Family poor, parents old, wife and children weak—through the seasons unable to sacrifice; drink, food, clothing insufficient to suit oneself—thus without shame, then there is nothing to compare to."
translations.idiomatic: Note: Sima Qian's preface to "Commodity Exchange" argues that when grinding poverty bars one from caring for parents or maintaining ritual, anyone who feels no shame over it cannot be judged alongside decent men.

sentences.zh: 無巖處奇士之行，而長貧賤，語仁義，亦足羞也。」

translations.literal: Without the conduct of cliff-dwelling extraordinary gentlemen, yet long poor and low—speaking benevolence and righteousness—is also sufficient shame."
translations.idiomatic: To lack the recluse's integrity yet linger in poverty while preaching morality—there is shame enough in that."

sentences.zh: 注[一０]史記遊俠傳序曰：「季次、原憲行君子之德，義不苟合當世，當世亦笑之。

translations.literal: Commentary [ten]: Records of the Grand Historian preface to Traditions of Roaming Bravoes says: "Jici and Yuan Xian practiced gentlemen's virtue—righteousness did not hastily conform to the age—the age also laughed at them.
translations.idiomatic: Note: In his preface to "Roaming Bravoes" Sima Qian cites Confucius's disciples Jici and Yuan Xian, whose stubborn integrity earned mockery from their contemporaries.

sentences.zh: 終身空室蓬戶，褐衣疏食不饜。

translations.literal: Their whole lives empty chambers, wicker gates—brown clothes, coarse food never satisfied.
translations.idiomatic: They lived out their days in bare rooms with wicker doors, coarse cloth and plain fare never enough to fill them.

sentences.zh: 今遊俠，其行雖不軌於正義，然其言必信，於行必果，已諾必誠，不愛其軀，赴士之□，蓋有足多者。

translations.literal: Now roaming bravoes—their conduct although not running in accordance with rectitude, yet their words must be trusted; in action must be decisive; promises once given must be sincere; not sparing their bodies—rushing to gentlemen's distress—probably there is enough to praise.
translations.idiomatic: Today's swordsmen may stray from classical righteousness, yet they keep faith, finish what they begin, honor every pledge, and hazard their lives answering gentlemen in distress—qualities Sima Qian finds genuinely admirable.

sentences.zh: 今拘學或抱咫尺之義，久孤於世，豈若卑論齊俗，與世沉浮而取榮名哉！」

translations.literal: Now cramped scholars perhaps embrace foot-rule righteousness—long isolated in the world—how compare to low discourse leveling custom, floating and sinking with the age and taking glory and fame!"
translations.idiomatic: Compared with pedants who clutch petty scruples and waste away unseen, is it not better to trim one's sails to the crowd and ride the age's currents to renown?"

sentences.zh: 注[一一]極刑謂遷被腐刑也。

translations.literal: Commentary [eleven]: Extreme punishment means Qian suffered castration punishment.
translations.idiomatic: Note: "Extreme punishment" refers to Sima Qian's sentence of castration.

sentences.zh: 遷與任安書曰：「最下腐刑，極矣！」

translations.literal: Qian's letter to Ren An says: "Lowest castration punishment—extreme!"
translations.idiomatic: As Sima Qian wrote to Ren An, castration was "the lowest" penalty—utter degradation.

sentences.zh: 注[一二]易曰：「顏氏之子，其殆庶幾乎！」

translations.literal: Commentary [twelve]: Changes says: "The son of the Yan clan—perhaps nearly there!"
translations.idiomatic: Note: The Zhouyi praises Yan Hui as one who "almost" attained sagehood.

sentences.zh: 夫百家之書，猶可法也。

translations.literal: Now the books of the hundred schools—still may be taken as models.
translations.idiomatic: Even the miscellaneous writings of the hundred schools retain lessons worth following.

sentences.zh: 若左氏、國語、世本、戰國策、楚漢春秋、太史公書，今之所以知古，後之所由觀前，聖人之耳目也。

translations.literal: Such as Zuo's tradition, Discourses of the States, Genealogical Origins, Intrigues of the Warring States, Spring and Autumn of Chu and Han, Grand Astrologer's Documents—the means by which today knows antiquity, whereby posterity observes the former—the ears and eyes of the sages.
translations.idiomatic: Works such as Zuo, Discourses of the States, Genealogical Origins, Intrigues of the Warring States, Lu Jia's Chu-Han chronicle, and Sima Qian's history are how we recover antiquity and how posterity watches our age—they are the senses of the tradition.

sentences.zh: 司馬遷序帝王則曰本紀，公侯傳國則曰世家，卿士特起則曰列傳。

translations.literal: Sima Qian ordering emperors and kings then calls them Basic Annals; dukes and marquises transmitting states then calls Hereditary Houses; ministers and grand officers specially arising then calls Arrayed Traditions.
translations.idiomatic: Sima Qian labeled imperial reigns "basic annals," hereditary noble houses "hereditary houses," and standalone ministers "arrayed traditions."

sentences.zh: 又進項羽、陳涉而黜淮南、衡山，[一]細意委曲，條列不經。

translations.literal: Again advancing Xiang Yu and Chen She while demoting Huainan and Hengshan; [one] detailed intention winding—listed ordering not canonical.
translations.idiomatic: He elevated Xiang Yu and Chen She to annals while relegating imperial princes of Huainan and Hengshan to mere biographies—[one] ingenious yet irregular categorizing.

sentences.zh: 若遷之著作，采獲古今，貫穿經傳，至廣博也。

translations.literal: As for Qian's writings—gathering ancient and modern, penetrating through classics and commentaries—extremely vast and broad.
translations.idiomatic: His compilation spans antiquity to his own day and threads every classical source—unsurpassed in breadth.

sentences.zh: 一人之精，文重思煩，故其書刊落不盡，尚有盈辭，多不齊一。

translations.literal: One man's essence—text heavy thought tangled—therefore his book carving and falling away not complete—still having surplus phrases—many not uniform.
translations.idiomatic: One scholar wrestling so vast a canvas inevitably left rough joints and uneven polish.

sentences.zh: [二]若序司馬相如，舉郡縣，著其字，至蕭、曹、陳平之屬，及董仲舒並時之人，不記其字，或縣而不郡者，蓋不暇也。

translations.literal: [Two] Such as ordering Sima Xiangru—raising commanderies and counties, writing his style-name—reaching Xiao, Cao, Chen Ping's sorts, and Dong Zhongshu—people of the same time—not recording their style-names, or county but not commandery—probably without leisure.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] He spells out counties for Sima Xiangru yet omits courtesy names for Xiao He, Cao Shen, Chen Ping, Dong Zhongshu, or marks only county seats—likely sheer overload.

sentences.zh: [三]今此後篇，慎核其事，整齊其文，不為世家，唯紀、傳而已。

translations.literal: [Three] Now this latter composition—carefully verifying its affairs, ordering its pattern—not making Hereditary Houses—only annals and traditions.
translations.idiomatic: [Three] My continuation will verify facts, tighten prose, and omit hereditary houses—only annals and biographies remain.

sentences.zh: 傳曰：「殺史見極，平易正直，春秋之義也。

translations.literal: Tradition says: "When rulers kill historians they expose their own extremity—easy, level, straightforward—the meaning of Spring and Autumn."
translations.idiomatic: The tradition warns that executing truthful chroniclers lays bare a regime's moral bankruptcy; plain, upright judgment is what Spring and Autumn stood for.

sentences.zh: 」 注[一]謂遷著項羽本紀。

translations.literal: " Commentary [one]: Means Qian composed Basic Annals of Xiang Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Note: This refers to Sima Qian placing Xiang Yu among the imperial annals.

sentences.zh: 又陳涉起於壟畝，數月被殺，無子孫相繼，著為世家，淮南、衡山，漢室之王胤，當世家而編之列傳，言進退之失也。

translations.literal: Again Chen She arose from ridge and furrow—within several months was killed—no descendants continuing—written as Hereditary House; Huainan, Hengshan—Han house's king descendants—should be Hereditary Houses yet compiled among Arrayed Traditions—speaks of advance-and-retreat loss.
translations.idiomatic: Chen She rose from the fields and died within months with no heirs, yet earned a hereditary house; imperial princes of Huainan and Hengshan deserved that slot yet were relegated to mere biographies—clear misjudgments of rank.

sentences.zh: 注[二]刊，削也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Kan means shave, cut away.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Here "carving" means trimming redundant wording.

sentences.zh: 謂削落繁蕪，仍有不盡。

translations.literal: Meaning shaving away luxuriant excess—still there is not exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: Even after cutting verbiage, excess remained.

sentences.zh: 注[三]史記「□青者，平陽人也」，「張釋之，堵陽人」，並不顯郡之類也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Records of the Grand Historian "□ Qing was a man of Pingyang," "Zhang Shizhi was a man of Duyang"—both are types not displaying commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Sima Qian gives merely the county for figures such as Wei Qing of Pingyang and Zhang Shizhi of Duyang without naming the commandery.

sentences.zh: 彪復辟司徒玉況府。

translations.literal: Ban Biao was again summoned to Minister of Education Yu Kuang's office.
translations.idiomatic: Ban Biao accepted another summons to serve on Minister of Education Yu Kuang's staff.

sentences.zh: [一]時東宮初建，諸王國並開，[二]而官屬未備，師保多闕。

translations.literal: [One] At that time the Eastern Palace was first established, the royal kingdoms were all opened; [two] yet office staffs were not complete, tutors and guardians mostly lacking.
translations.idiomatic: [One] The heir apparent's residence had just been founded and imperial princes had received their fiefs, [two] but their households lacked full complements of officials, especially preceptors and guardians.

sentences.zh: 彪上言曰： 注[一]玉音肅。

translations.literal: Ban Biao submitted a memorial saying: Commentary [one]: Yu is pronounced like su.
translations.idiomatic: Ban submitted a memorial. Note: The surname Yu here rhymes with su.

sentences.zh: 注[二]建武二十三年玉況為司徒，十九年建明帝為太子，十七年封諸王。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Jianwu twenty-third year Yu Kuang became Minister of Education; nineteenth year established Ming Emperor as heir; seventeenth year enfeoffed the kings.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Yu Kuang became Minister of Education in Jianwu 23; the crown prince was installed in Jianwu 19; princely kingdoms were created in Jianwu 17.

sentences.zh: 注[一]見論語。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: See Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Note: See the Analects.

sentences.zh: 注[二]賈誼上疏之辭。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Language from Jia Yi's submitted memorial.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Quoting Jia Yi's memorial language.

sentences.zh: 注[三]左傳曰：「自郊勞至於贈賄，禮無違者。」

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Zuo's Tradition says: "From suburban greeting to parting gift of silk, in ritual none violated."
translations.idiomatic: Note: Zuo's Commentary says courtesy ran flawlessly from the greeting outside the capital to the farewell gifts.

sentences.zh: 注[四]左傳□大夫石碏諫□莊公之辭也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Zuo's Tradition □ grand officer Shi Que remonstrating □ Duke Zhuang's words.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The remonstrance of Minister Shi Que to Duke Zhuang of Wei in Zuo's Commentary.

sentences.zh: 注[五]詩大雅也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: It is an ode of the Greater Ya of Poetry.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The passage comes from the Greater Odes of the Classic of Poetry.

sentences.zh: 詒，遺也。

translations.literal: Yi means leave behind, bequeath.
translations.idiomatic: Yi here means "to bequeath."

sentences.zh: 宴，安也。

translations.literal: Yan means peaceful.
translations.idiomatic: Yan means "peace" or "ease."

sentences.zh: 翼，敬也。

translations.literal: Yi means reverent.
translations.idiomatic: Yi glosses as "reverent care."

sentences.zh: 言文王遺其孫以善謀，武王以安敬之道遺其子。

translations.literal: It says King Wen left his grandson good planning; King Wu with peaceful reverent way left his son.
translations.idiomatic: King Wen left his grandson sound counsel; King Wu left his son the ways of peace and reverence.

sentences.zh: 子謂成王也。

translations.literal: Son means King Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: The "son" is King Cheng.

sentences.zh: 漢興，太宗使□錯導太子以法術，[一]賈誼教梁王以詩書。

translations.literal: When Han arose, Emperor Taizong had □ Chao guide the heir apparent with legal methods; [one] Jia Yi taught the Prince of Liang with Poetry and Documents.
translations.idiomatic: Early Han saw Emperor Wen commission Chao Cuo to instruct the crown prince in statute law while Jia Yi tutored the Prince of Liang in the Classics.

sentences.zh: [二]及至中宗，亦令劉向、王□、蕭望之、周堪之徒，以文章儒學保訓東宮以下，[三]莫不崇簡其人，就成德器。

translations.literal: [Two] Reaching Emperor Zhongzong, also ordered Liu Xiang, Wang □, Xiao Wangzhi, Zhou Kan's sorts—using literary achievement Confucian learning to guard and instruct the Eastern Palace downward; [three] none did not esteem select persons—thereby completing virtuous vessels.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] Under Emperor Xuan, tutors such as Liu Xiang, Wang Bao, Xiao Wangzhi, and Zhou Kan shaped the heir apparent's household through scholarship; [three] each appointment weighed moral substance.

sentences.zh: 今皇太子諸王，雖結髮學問，修習禮樂，而傅相未值賢才，官屬多闕舊典。

translations.literal: Now crown prince and kings—although tying hair study inquiry, cultivate practice ritual and music—yet tutors and chancellors have not met worthy talents; office staffs mostly lack old canons.
translations.idiomatic: Today's heir apparent and princes pursue ritual study from youth, yet their tutors and ministers are not always men of talent, and household offices fall short of classical precedent.

sentences.zh: 宜博選名儒有威重明通政事者，以為太子太傅，東宮及諸王國，備置官屬。

translations.literal: It is fitting broadly to select famed Confucians who have awesome weight and clear penetration of governing affairs—to act as grand tutor to the heir apparent; Eastern Palace and the royal kingdoms—fully establish office staffs.
translations.idiomatic: Choose eminent scholars of gravity and administrative insight as grand tutor to the crown prince and fill out every office for the Eastern Palace and each princely establishment.

sentences.zh: 又舊制，太子食湯沐十縣，設周□交戟，五日一朝，因坐東箱，省視膳食，其非朝日，使僕、中允旦旦請問而已，明不媟黷，廣其敬也。

translations.literal: Again old system: heir apparent eats soup-bath ten counties; establish Zhou □ crossed halberds; five days one court audience—thereupon sit eastern wing—inspect view drink food; those not audience days—cause steward, palace attendant morning morning inquire ask only—clarify not lewd familiarity—broaden his reverence.
translations.idiomatic: By precedent the heir drew revenue from ten counties for bath grants, guards bore crossed halberds, and he greeted his father every five days from the eastern corridor while inspecting the imperial kitchen; on other mornings only stewards and junior attendants carried routine greetings—protocol meant to prevent undue intimacy while deepening respect.

sentences.zh: [四] 注[一]文帝時□錯為博士，上言曰：「人主所以顯功揚名者，以知術數也。

translations.literal: [Four] Commentary [one]: In Emperor Wen's time □ Chao was erudite; submitted memorial saying: "The reason the lord of men displays merit and raises fame is by knowing techniques and calculations."
translations.idiomatic: [Four] Note: As erudite under Emperor Wen, Chao Cuo argued that a ruler's renown rests on mastering statecraft.

sentences.zh: 今皇太子所讀書多矣，而未知術數。

translations.literal: Now crown prince's read books are many—yet does not know techniques and calculations.
translations.idiomatic: The heir reads widely yet lacks practical administrative training.

sentences.zh: 願陛下擇聖人之術以賜太子。

translations.literal: I wish Your Majesty select sages' techniques to grant crown prince.
translations.idiomatic: Pray choose sage-approved methods to instruct him.

sentences.zh: 」上善之，拜錯為太子家令。

translations.literal: " The emperor deemed it good—appointed Chao as steward of crown prince's household.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor approved and named Chao Cuo steward of the heir's household.

sentences.zh: 注[二]賈誼為梁王太傅。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Jia Yi was grand tutor to Prince of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Jia Yi served as grand tutor to the Prince of Liang.

sentences.zh: 梁王，文帝之少子，名揖，愛而好書，故令誼傅之。

translations.literal: Prince of Liang—Emperor Wen's youngest son, named Yi—loved and fond of documents—therefore ordered Yi tutor him.
translations.idiomatic: The prince was Emperor Wen's youngest son, Yi, bookish and beloved—hence Jia Yi's appointment.

sentences.zh: 注[三]中宗，宣帝也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Zhongzong is Emperor Xuan.
translations.idiomatic: Note: "Zhongzong" here means Emperor Xuan.

sentences.zh: 時元帝為太子，宣帝使王□、劉向、張子僑等之太子宮，娛侍太子朝夕讀誦，蕭望之為太傅，周堪為少傅。

translations.literal: At that time Emperor Yuan was heir—Emperor Xuan caused Wang □, Liu Xiang, Zhang Ziqiao's sorts to go to heir's palace—amusing serving heir morning evening read recite—Xiao Wangzhi was grand tutor—Zhou Kan was junior tutor.
translations.idiomatic: While Yuan was heir, Xuan detailed Wang Bao, Liu Xiang, Zhang Ziqiao, and others to the Eastern Palace for daily instruction; Xiao Wangzhi served as grand tutor and Zhou Kan as junior tutor.

sentences.zh: 並見前書。

translations.literal: All seen in former book.
translations.idiomatic: See the earlier Han History.

sentences.zh: 注[四]漢官儀曰：「皇太子五日一至台，因坐東箱，省視膳食，以法制□太官尚食宰吏，其非朝日，使僕、中允旦旦請問，明不媟黷，所以廣敬也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four] Han Official Ceremonial says: "Crown prince once every five days comes once to terrace—thereupon sits eastern wing—inspect drink food—by legal system □ grand provisioner chief food steward clerks—those not audience days—cause steward, palace attendant morning morning inquire ask—clarify not lewd familiarity—thereby broaden reverence."
translations.idiomatic: Note: The Han Official Ceremonial records that the heir attended court every five days from the eastern gallery to review the imperial kitchen; stewards supervised the palace catering office by statute, and on non-audience days only household stewards and junior attendants carried greetings—keeping ritual distance to deepen respect.

sentences.zh: 太子僕一人，秩千石；

translations.literal: Steward of the heir—one man, rank one thousand piculs;
translations.idiomatic: One steward of the heir apparent at one thousand piculs;

sentences.zh: 中允一人，四百石，主門□徼巡。」

translations.literal: Palace attendant—one man, four hundred piculs—supervises gate □ patrol inspection."
translations.idiomatic: one junior attendant at four hundred piculs overseeing gate patrols and rounds."

sentences.zh: 書奏，帝納之。

translations.literal: Memorial submitted—emperor accepted it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor endorsed Ban's memorial.

sentences.zh: 後察司徒廉為望都長，吏民愛之。

translations.literal: Later investigated Minister of Education integrity—became magistrate of Wangdu—clerks and common people loved him.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended as an honest official by the Minister of Education, he became magistrate of Wangdu and won the people's affection.

sentences.zh: [一]建武三十年，年五十二，卒官。

translations.literal: [One] Jianwu thirtieth year—age fifty-two—died in office.
translations.idiomatic: [One] He died in office at fifty-two in Jianwu 30 (54 CE).

sentences.zh: 所著賦、論、書、記、奏事合九篇。

translations.literal: His composed rhapsodies, discourses, letters, records, memorials altogether nine scrolls.
translations.idiomatic: His collected fu, essays, letters, records, and memorials fill nine pieces.

sentences.zh: 注[一]察，舉也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Cha means raise up, recommend.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Cha means "recommended for office."

sentences.zh: 司徒薦為廉。

translations.literal: Minister of Education recommended as integrity.
translations.idiomatic: The Minister of Education nominated him as an "honest" candidate.

sentences.zh: 二子：固，超。

translations.literal: Two sons: Gu, Chao.
translations.idiomatic: He had two sons: Ban Gu and Ban Chao.

sentences.zh: 超別有傳。

translations.literal: Chao separately has tradition.
translations.idiomatic: Ban Chao has his own biography elsewhere.

sentences.zh: 論曰：班彪以通儒上才，傾側危亂之閒，行不踰方，[一]言不失正，仕不急進，貞不違人，敷文華以緯國典，守賤薄而無悶容。

translations.literal: Discussion says: Ban Biao by thoroughly Confucian superior talent—in leaning tilting peril chaos between—conduct did not transgress square; [one] speech did not lose correctness—office did not urgent advance—chaste did not oppose others—spread literary splendor to weave state canons—guarding mean low yet without depressed appearance.
translations.idiomatic: Assessed thus: Ban Biao united encyclopedic learning with personal courage amid turmoil—his conduct stayed within moral bounds, [one] his counsel stayed upright, he neither scrambled for promotion nor compromised others, he draped state ritual with fine prose, and bore humble posts without resentment.

sentences.zh: 彼將以世運未弘，非所謂賤焉恥乎？

translations.literal: Would they take age's fortune not vast—not what is called mean thereby shame?
translations.idiomatic: Should we infer he felt no shame in poverty because the age had not yet fulfilled its promise?

sentences.zh: 何其守道恬淡之篤也！

translations.literal: How his guarding Way tranquil bland depth!
translations.idiomatic: How steadfast his quiet devotion to the Way!

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]論語孔子曰：「可謂仁之方。

translations.literal: [Two] Commentary [one]: Analects Confucius says: "Can be called benevolence's direction.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] Note: Confucius speaks of "the method of benevolence."

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注云：「方猶道也。」

translations.literal: " Zheng Xuan's commentary says: "Fang is like Way."
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan glosses fang as "the Way."

sentences.zh: 注[二]孔子曰：「邦有道，貧且賤焉恥也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Confucius says: "When state has Way—poor moreover low thereby shame.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Confucius said shame attaches to poverty only when the state is well governed.

sentences.zh: 」言彪當中興之初，時運未泰，故不以貧賤為恥，何守道清靜之固也！

translations.literal: " Speaks Ban Biao faced restoration's beginning—times' fortune not peaceful—therefore did not take poverty low as shame—how guarding Way clear quiet solidity!
translations.idiomatic: Ban lived at the dynasty's revival when fortune still hung in the balance, so modest rank carried no stigma—another measure of his serene fidelity.

sentences.zh: 恬淡猶清靜也。

translations.literal: Tranquil bland is like clear quiet.
translations.idiomatic: "Tranquil" here means quietly detached.

sentences.zh: 篤，固也。

translations.literal: Du means solid.
translations.idiomatic: Du means "firm" or "deep-rooted."

sentences.zh: 固字孟堅。

translations.literal: Gu's style-name was Mengjian.
translations.idiomatic: Ban Gu, courtesy name Mengjian.

sentences.zh: 年九歲，能屬文誦詩賦，及長，遂博貫載籍，九流百家之言，無不窮究。

translations.literal: Age nine years—able compose texts recite rhapsodies—reaching grown—thereupon broadly penetrating carrying texts—nine currents hundred schools' words—none not exhaustively investigated.
translations.idiomatic: At nine he could compose prose and chant poetry; as an adult he mastered the canonical corpus and explored every school among the nine traditions.

sentences.zh: [一]所學無常師，不為章句，舉大義而已。

translations.literal: [One] What he studied had no constant teacher—did not make chapter-sentence—raised great meanings only.
translations.idiomatic: [One] He studied without binding himself to a single master, eschewed philological hair-splitting, and pursued overarching significance.

sentences.zh: 性寬和容觿，不以才能高人，諸儒以此慕之。

translations.literal: By nature lenient harmonizing containing multitudes—not by talent ability raise above people—various Confucians thereby admired him.
translations.idiomatic: Genial and inclusive, he never flaunted his gifts, and scholars everywhere admired him for it.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]九流謂道﹑儒﹑墨﹑名﹑法﹑陰陽﹑農﹑雜﹑縱橫。

translations.literal: [Two] Commentary [one]: Nine currents means Dao, Confucian, Mohist, Logician, Legalist, Yin-Yang, Agriculturist, Eclectic, Alliance-and-defiance.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] Note: The "nine traditions" are Daoist, Confucian, Mohist, Sophist, Legalist, Yin-Yang, Agriculturist, Syncretist, and Diplomatist schools.

sentences.zh: 注[二]謝承書曰：「固年十三，王充見之，拊其背謂彪曰：『此兒必記漢事。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Xie Cheng's History says: "When Gu was thirteen, Wang Chong saw him—patted his back saying to Ban Biao: 'This boy will surely record Han affairs.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note: Xie Cheng records that at thirteen Ban Gu impressed Wang Chong, who told Ban Biao the boy would one day write Han history.

sentences.zh: 』」永平初，東平王蒼以至戚為驃騎將軍輔政，開東合，延英雄。

translations.literal: '" Early Yongping, Prince of Dongping Cang by utmost kinship became general of agile cavalry assisting governance—opened eastern compound—extended heroes.
translations.idiomatic: Early in the Yongping era (58–75 CE), Liu Cang, prince of Dongping, served as regent as general of agile cavalry and drew worthy men to his eastern bureau.

sentences.zh: 時固始弱冠，奏記說蒼曰：[一] 注[一]奏，進也。

translations.literal: At that time Gu had just capped weak crown—submitted memorial persuading Cang saying: [one] Commentary [one]: Zou means advance.
translations.idiomatic: Ban Gu, newly capped, presented a letter of counsel to Liu Cang. Note: Zou means "to submit" or "present."

sentences.zh: 記，書也。

translations.literal: Ji means document.
translations.idiomatic: Ji denotes a formal letter.

sentences.zh: 前書待詔鄭朋奏記於蕭望之，奏記自朋始也。

translations.literal: Former book awaiting edict Zheng Peng submitted memorial to Xiao Wangzhi—from Peng began submitting memorial.
translations.idiomatic: The History of Western Han credits Zheng Peng with pioneering this genre when he addressed Xiao Wangzhi.

sentences.zh: 將軍以周﹑邵之德，立乎本朝，承休明之策，建威靈之號，[一]昔在周公，今也將軍，詩書所載，未有三此者也。

translations.literal: General by Zhou-Shao's virtue—stands in this court—inherits peaceful bright plans—establishes awesome numinous title [one]—formerly was Duke of Zhou—now is general—Poetry and Documents record—not yet thrice this.
translations.idiomatic: You unite the Duke of Zhou's virtue with the Duke of Shao's statesmanship at court, inherit an enlightened mandate, and bear the awe-inspiring title of general of agile cavalry—[one] the Classics pair only the Duke of Zhou with such honor until yourself.

sentences.zh: [二]傳曰：「必有非常之人，然後有非常之事；

translations.literal: [Two] Tradition says: "Surely there must be extraordinary persons—afterward there are extraordinary affairs;
translations.idiomatic: [Two] The tradition runs: extraordinary deeds demand extraordinary men;

sentences.zh: 有非常之事，然後有非常之功。

translations.literal: have extraordinary affairs—afterward there are extraordinary achievements.
translations.idiomatic: extraordinary deeds in turn yield extraordinary merit.

sentences.zh: 」[三]固幸得生於清明之世，豫在視聽之末，私以螻蟻，竊觀國政，[四] 誠美將軍擁千載之任，躡先聖之蹤，[五]體弘懿之姿，據高明之埶，博貫庶事，服膺六蓺，白黑簡心，求善無猒，[六]采擇狂夫之言，不逆負薪之議。

translations.literal: " [Three] Gu fortunately attained birth in clear bright age—participating in seeing-hearing's end—privately as ant—secretly observe state governance—[four] truly praise general embracing thousand-year's task—tread former sage's tracks—[five] embody vast excellent bearing—occupy high bright situation—broadly penetrate myriad affairs—cherish breast six arts—white black simple heart—seek good without satiety—[six] gather choose mad man's words—not oppose firewood-carrier's discussions.
translations.idiomatic: " [Three] I am privileged to live in this enlightened age and, though no more than an ant among your listeners, [four] admire how you shoulder a millennial charge and walk in the Duke of Zhou's footsteps—[five] gracious yet commanding, omnicompetent yet steeped in the six classics, discerning good and evil as plainly as black and white, insatiable for wise counsel—[six] welcoming blunt speech and weighing woodcutter analogies.

sentences.zh: [七]竊見幕府新開，廣延腢俊，四方之士，顛倒衣裳。

translations.literal: [Seven] Secretly see headquarters newly opened—broadly extend talented worthies—four directions' gentlemen—topsy-turvy robes skirts.
translations.idiomatic: [Seven] Your new headquarters already gathers brilliant men from every quarter—they scramble to answer your summons.

sentences.zh: [八]將軍宜詳唐﹑殷之舉，察伊﹑戲之薦，[九]令遠近無偏，幽隱必達，期於總覽賢才，收集明智，為國得人，以寧本朝。

translations.literal: [Eight] General ought examine Tang-Yin's selections—investigate Yi-Xi's recommendations—[nine] cause far near without bias—hidden secluded must reach—hope to gather overview worthy talents—collect gather bright wisdom—for state obtain men—thereby pacify this court.
translations.idiomatic: [Eight] Study how Tang and Yin promoted talent and how Yi Yin and Xi Shi were discovered—[nine] leave no corner unseen so that every worthy serves the state and secures the dynasty.

sentences.zh: 則將軍養志和神，優遊廟堂，光名宣於當世，遺烈著於無窮。

translations.literal: Then general nurture intent harmonize spirit—leisurely roam temple hall—bright fame proclaim in present age—left fierce shining in boundless.
translations.idiomatic: You may cultivate mind and body at ease within the halls of power, win acclaim in your own day, and leave fame undying.

sentences.zh: 注[一]號驃騎將軍也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The title is general of agile cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The grand title is general of agile cavalry.

sentences.zh: 注[二]唯蒼與周公二人而已。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Only Cang and Duke of Zhou—two persons alone.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Only Liu Cang and the Duke of Zhou share such stature.

sentences.zh: 注[三]司馬相如喻蜀之辭。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Language from Sima Xiangru's Shu analogy.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Quoting Sima Xiangru's rhetoric on Shu.

sentences.zh: 注[四]螻蟻謂細微也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Ant means subtle slight.
translations.idiomatic: Note: "Ant" underscores Ban Gu's humble station.

sentences.zh: 注[五]千載謂自周公至明帝時千餘載也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Thousand-year means from Duke of Zhou to Ming Emperor times above thousand years.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The thousand-year span runs from the Duke of Zhou to Emperor Ming.

sentences.zh: 先聖謂周公也。

translations.literal: Former sage means Duke of Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: "Former sage" denotes the Duke of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 注[六]淮南子曰：「聖人見是非，若白黑之別於目。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: Huainanzi says: "Sage sees right wrong—like white black's distinction in eyes.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Huainanzi compares the sage's moral clarity to distinguishing black from white.

sentences.zh: 」左傳曰「求善不□」也。

translations.literal: " Zuo's Tradition says "seek good not □" also.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo's Commentary urges relentless pursuit of moral excellence.

sentences.zh: 注[七]負薪，賤人也。

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: Carrying firewood—mean persons.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Woodcutters ranked among common laborers.

sentences.zh: 三略曰「負新之諾，廊廟之言」也。

translations.literal: Three Strategies says "carrying new firewood's promise—hall temple's words" also.
translations.idiomatic: The Three Strategies couples woodland pledges with counsel fit for the throne—the phrase praises weighing humble speech.

sentences.zh: 注[八]詩曰：「東方未明，顛倒衣裳。

translations.literal: Commentary [eight]: Poetry says: "East not yet bright—topsy-turvy robe skirt.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The Classic of Poetry describes scholars stumbling half dressed to serve.

sentences.zh: 」言士爭歸之□遽也。

translations.literal: " Speaks gentlemen competing return it □ hastily.
translations.idiomatic: Image stresses how eagerly gentlemen rushed to join him.

sentences.zh: 注[九]堯舉戲陶，湯舉伊尹。

translations.literal: Commentary [nine]: Yao raised Xi Tao—Tang raised Yi Yin.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Yao elevated worthy men such as Xi Tao; Tang appointed Yi Yin.

sentences.zh: 竊見故司空掾桓梁，宿儒盛名，冠德州裡，七十從心，行不踰矩，[一]蓋清廟之光暉，當世之俊彥也。

translations.literal: Secretly see former Minister of Works aide Huan Liang—lodged Confucian great fame—caps virtue district—seventy follow heart—conduct not transgress square—[one] likely pure temple's radiance—present age's outstanding talent.
translations.idiomatic: I note Huan Liang, formerly clerk to the Minister of Works—a revered scholar whose reputation tops his region; past seventy he follows his heart yet breaks no rule—[one] truly the gleam of the royal shrine, a paragon of our day.

sentences.zh: [二]京兆祭酒晉馮，結髮修身，白首無違，好古樂道，玄默自守，古人之美行，時俗所莫及。

translations.literal: [Two] Jingzhao libationer Jin Feng—tying hair cultivating body—white head without violation—fond antiquity delight Way—dark silent self guard—ancients' beautiful conduct—present vulgar cannot reach.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] Jin Feng, libationer of Jingzhao, has cultivated himself since youth without lapsing in old age; he loves antiquity and the Way, lives quietly, and embodies virtues beyond ordinary fashion.

sentences.zh: 扶風掾李育，[三]經明行著，教授百人，客居杜陵，茅室土階。

translations.literal: Fufeng aide Li Yu—[three] classics bright conduct manifest—instructs hundred people—guest lodges Duling—thatched room earthen steps.
translations.idiomatic: Li Yu of Fufeng commands the classics, trains a hundred disciples, and rents a hut with earthen steps at Duling—[three] scholarship made visible in conduct.

sentences.zh: 京兆﹑扶風二郡更請，徒以家貧，數辭病去。

translations.literal: Jingzhao—Fufeng two commanderies alternate invite—only because family poor—repeatedly decline illness depart.
translations.idiomatic: Both commanderies sought him again and again; poverty alone drove him to plead illness and withdraw.

sentences.zh: 溫故知新，論議通明，廉清修絜，行能純備，雖前世名儒，國家所器，韋﹑平﹑孔﹑翟，無以加焉。

translations.literal: Warm old know new—discussion debate transparent bright—incorrupt pure cultivated pure—conduct ability purely complete—although former age famed Confucians—state treasured—Wei—Ping—Kong—Zhai—none can add thereto.
translations.idiomatic: Reviewing the past and discerning the new, lucid in debate, incorrupt and meticulous, he rivals the Han state's greatest scholars—Wei, Ping, Kong, and Zhai included.

sentences.zh: [四] 宜令考續，以參萬事。

translations.literal: [Four] Should order examine succession—to participate myriad affairs.
translations.idiomatic: [Four] Summon him for evaluation so he may advise on every branch of administration.

sentences.zh: 京兆督郵郭基，孝行著於州裡，經學稱於師門，政務之績，有絕異之效。

translations.literal: Jingzhao supervising postal Guo Ji—filial conduct manifest in district—classics learning praised at teacher gate—administrative affairs' traces—have surpassing unusual efficacy.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Ji of Jingzhao supervision is famed locally for filial piety, excels in classical studies under noted teachers, and delivers administrative results of rare distinction.

sentences.zh: 如得及明時，秉事下僚，進有羽翮奮翔之用，退有□梁一介之死。

translations.literal: If obtain reach bright times—hold affairs lower officials—advance has feather wing soar use—retreat has □ beam one man's death.
translations.idiomatic: Given timely employment even at modest rank, he would lend wings in ascent yet pledge his life like a loyal beam should duty demand it.

sentences.zh: [五]涼州從事王雍，躬卞嚴之節，文之以術蓺，[六]涼州冠蓋，未有宜先雍者也。

translations.literal: [Five] Liangzhou aide Wang Yong—personally Bian Zhuang's integrity—pattern it with arts skills—[six] Liangzhou caps covers—none yet ought precede Yong.
translations.idiomatic: [Five] Wang Yong, aide in Liangzhou, embodies the steadfast honor of Bian Zhuang and adorns it with learning—[six] among Liangzhou notables none ranks ahead of him.

sentences.zh: 古者周公一舉則三方怨，曰「奚為而後己」。

translations.literal: Ancient times Duke Zhou one raise then three regions resent—saying "why act afterward self".
translations.idiomatic: When Duke Zhou elevated one candidate, three domains complained he stopped too soon.

sentences.zh: [七]宜及府開，以慰遠方。

translations.literal: [Seven] Should reach headquarters open—to comfort far regions.
translations.idiomatic: [Seven] Act while your bureau opens its doors to reassure the distant frontier.

sentences.zh: 弘農功曹史殷肅，[八]達學洽聞，才能絕倫，誦詩三百，奉使專對。

translations.literal: Hongnong merit clerk Yin Su—[eight] penetrating learning broad hearing—talent ability unmatched—recite Poetry three hundred—receive mission specialized reply.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Su of Hongnong combines encyclopedic learning with diplomatic flair—he knows the three hundred odes by heart and can debate extemporaneously on embassy—[eight].

sentences.zh: 此六子者，皆有殊行絕才，德隆當世，如蒙徵納，以輔高明，此山梁之秋，夫子所為歎也。

translations.literal: These six gentlemen—all have unusual conduct surpassing talent—virtue lofty present age—if receive summons inclusion—to assist high bright—this ridge autumn—Master thereby sighed.
translations.idiomatic: These six combine singular virtue with outstanding talent; should you enlist them to aid your enlightened rule, you echo the sigh Confucius breathed over the ridge stag.

sentences.zh: [九]昔卞和獻寶，以離斷趾，[一０]靈均納忠，終於沉身，[一一]而和氏之璧，千載垂光，屈子之篇，萬世歸善。

translations.literal: [Nine] Former Bian He presented treasure—thereby suffering cut toes—[ten] Ling Jun presented loyalty—ended in drowned body—[eleven] yet He's jade—thousand years hangs radiance—Qu Yuan's chapters—ten thousand generations return good.
translations.idiomatic: [Nine] Bian He lost his feet presenting his jade—[ten] Qu Yuan drowned offering loyalty—[eleven] yet the He's disk still shines after ages and Qu Yuan's poems teach goodness forever.

sentences.zh: 願將軍隆照微之明，信日□之聽，[一二]少屈威神，咨嗟下問，令塵埃之中，永無荊山﹑汨羅之恨。

translations.literal: Wish general lofty illuminate subtle brightness—trust sun-bright hearing—[twelve] slightly bend awesome spirit—sigh inquire lower ask—cause amid dust—eternally without Jing Mountain Miluo regret.
translations.idiomatic: Pray lend discerning light and hearing as clear as noon—[twelve] humble your formidable dignity long enough to inquire below—so talent buried in dust never repeats Jing Mountain's jade or Miluo's drowned poet.

sentences.zh: 注[一]論語孔子曰：「七十而縱心所欲，不踰矩。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Analects Confucius says: "Seventy yet follow heart what desires—not transgress square.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Confucius says at seventy one follows inclination yet stays within bounds.

sentences.zh: 」言恣心之所為，皆闇合於法則。

translations.literal: " Speaks indulge heart's what acts—all dark match with rules.
translations.idiomatic: Every impulse already harmonizes with moral law.

sentences.zh: 注[二]詩周頌曰：「於穆清廟，肅雍顯相，濟濟多士，執文之德。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Poetry Zhou hymn says: "Ah solemn pure temple—solemn harmonious manifest assist—many gentlemen—hold pattern's virtue."
translations.idiomatic: Note: The Zhou hymn "Pure Temple" praises solemn ministers embodying patterned virtue.

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注曰：「顯，光也。

translations.literal: " Zheng Xuan commentary says: "Xian means radiance."
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan glosses xian as "radiance."

sentences.zh: 」言桓梁可參多士，助祭於清廟為光暉也。

translations.literal: " Speaking Huan Liang may join the host of officers—assist sacrifice at the Pure Temple—thus becoming radiance.
translations.idiomatic: The line ranks Huan Liang among the ministers who serve the royal ancestral shrine—the glory invoked in the hymn.

sentences.zh: 爾雅曰：「髦，俊也。

translations.literal: Erya says: "Mao means outstanding."
translations.idiomatic: The Erya glosses mao as "preeminent talent."

sentences.zh: 」美士為彥。

translations.literal: " Fine gentlemen are called yan.
translations.idiomatic: The text calls exemplary gentlemen yan.

sentences.zh: 注[三]育字符春，見儒林傳。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Yu's courtesy name is Zichun—see Forest of Scholars tradition.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Li Yu's courtesy name was Zichun—see his biography under Confucian Scholars.

sentences.zh: 注[四]韋賢﹑平當﹑孔光﹑翟方進也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Wei Xian, Ping Dang, Kong Guang, Zhai Fangjin.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The luminaries meant are Wei Xian, Ping Dang, Kong Guang, and Zhai Fangjin.

sentences.zh: 流俗本「平」字作「玄」，誤。

translations.literal: Vulgar editions write the character ping as xuan—mistake.
translations.idiomatic: Popular texts wrongly substitute xuan for ping.

sentences.zh: 注[五]說苑曰：「趙簡子游於西河而歎曰：『安得賢士而與處焉？

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Garden of Persuasions says: "Marquis Jian of Zhao travelled West River and sighed saying: 'How obtain worthy gentlemen and dwell with them?
translations.idiomatic: Note: Tales from the Garden of Persuasions relates how Marquis Jian of Zhao sighed by the western Yellow River, longing for worthy companions.

sentences.zh: 』舟人吉桑對曰：『鴻鵠高飛，所恃者六翮也。

translations.literal: " Boatman Ji Sang replied saying: 'Wild goose swan high soar—what relies on are six wing-shafts.
translations.idiomatic: Boatsman Ji Sang answered that wild swans depend on their flight feathers.

sentences.zh: 背上之毛，腹下之毳，加之滿把，飛不能為之益高。

translations.literal: Back's fur—belly's down—add them full handful—flying cannot thereby increase height.
translations.idiomatic: The fluff on back and belly, handfuls of it, adds nothing to altitude.

sentences.zh: 不知門下左右客千人，亦有六翮之用乎？

translations.literal: Do not know under gate left-right guests thousand persons—also have six wing-shafts' use?
translations.idiomatic: Among your thousand retainers, how many possess real wing feathers?

sentences.zh: 將盡毛毳也？

translations.literal: Will they all be fur down?
translations.idiomatic: Or are they mere fluff?

sentences.zh: 』」又曰「齊莊公攻莒，□梁與華周進□，壞軍陷陣，三軍不敢當。

translations.literal: " Again says "Qi Duke Zhuang attacked Ju—Xi Liang and Hua Zhou spearheaded assault—broke army pierced formation—three armies dared not withstand.
translations.idiomatic: Another tale describes Zhuang of Qi attacking Ju—Xi Liang and Hua Zhou spearheaded assaults no army could withstand.

sentences.zh: 至莒城下，殺二十七人而死」也。

translations.literal: Reaching Ju city's below—killed twenty-seven persons then died" also.
translations.idiomatic: They fought to Ju's walls, slew twenty-seven foes, and died—a measure of valor cited earlier.

sentences.zh: 注[六]卞嚴，卞莊子也。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: Bian Yan is Bian Zhuangzi.
translations.idiomatic: Note: "Bian Yan" here refers to Bian Zhuangzi.

sentences.zh: 新序曰：「卞莊子好勇，養母，戰而三北，交遊非之，國君辱之。

translations.literal: New Prefaces says: "Bian Zhuangzi fond bravery—nurtured mother—battled thrice fled north—companions blamed him—state lord shamed him.
translations.idiomatic: New Prefaces tells how Bian Zhuangzi loved valor yet fled thrice to support his mother—friends scorned him and his lord shamed him.

sentences.zh: 莊子受命，顏色不變。

translations.literal: Zhuangzi received mandate—color did not change.
translations.idiomatic: He bore the reproach without flinching.

sentences.zh: 及母死三年，齊與魯戰，莊子請從。

translations.literal: When mother died three years—Qi with Lu battled—Zhuangzi requested follow.
translations.idiomatic: After mourning three years, when Qi fought Lu, he begged to join the host.

sentences.zh: 至，見於將軍曰：『初獨與母處，是以戰而三北。

translations.literal: Arriving—seen by general saying: 'First alone dwelt with mother—thereby battled thrice fled north.
translations.idiomatic: Before the general he explained that earlier duty to his mother alone had forced three retreats.

sentences.zh: 今母沒矣，請塞責。

translations.literal: Now mother gone—request block blame.
translations.idiomatic: Now she was gone—he asked to redeem his honor.

sentences.zh: 』遂赴敵而□，獲甲首而獻，曰：『夫三北，以養母也。

sentences.zh: 太華，山也，山海經曰，華首之西六十里曰太華。

translations.literal: Grand Hua—mountain—Classic of Mountains and Seas says—Hua head's west sixty li called Grand Hua.
translations.idiomatic: Mount Taihua lies sixty li west of Huashou according to the Classic of Mountains and Seas.

sentences.zh: 終南，長安南山也。

translations.literal: Zhongnan—Chang'an southern mountain.
translations.idiomatic: Zhongnan is Chang'an's southern barrier.

sentences.zh: 詩曰：「終南何有。

translations.literal: Poetry says: "Zhongnan what has.
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Poetry asks "What grows on Zhongnan?"

sentences.zh: 」注云：「終南，周之名山中南也。」

translations.literal: " Commentary says: "Zhongnan—Zhou's famed mountain central south."
translations.idiomatic: A gloss calls Zhongnan Zhou's celebrated southern peak.

sentences.zh: 注[三]□斜，谷名，南口曰□，北口曰斜，在今梁州。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Bao-Xie—valley name—south mouth called Bao—north mouth called Xie—in present Liangzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The Bao-Xie valley opens south at Bao and north at Xie in modern Liangzhou.

sentences.zh: 隴首，山名，在今秦州。

translations.literal: Longtou—mountain name—in present Qinzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Longtou is a hill name in modern Qinzhou.

sentences.zh: 洪，大也。

translations.literal: Hong means great.
translations.idiomatic: Hong means "vast" (of the river).

sentences.zh: 注[四]華實之毛謂草木也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Flower fruit's down means grass trees.
translations.idiomatic: Note: "Down of flower and fruit" denotes vegetation.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「食土之毛。

translations.literal: Zuo's Tradition says: "Eat earth's down.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo's Commentary speaks of living off the land's produce.

sentences.zh: 」前書曰：「秦地九州膏腴。

translations.literal: " Former book says: "Qin territory nine provinces fertile.
translations.idiomatic: The History of Western Han calls Qin soil among the empire's richest.

sentences.zh: 」尚書雍州「厥田上上」。

translations.literal: " Documents Yongzhou "its fields upper upper."
translations.idiomatic: The Documents ranks Yongzhou's fields highest grade.

sentences.zh: 防禦謂關禁也。

translations.literal: Defense barrier means pass prohibition.
translations.idiomatic: "Defense" means guarded passes.

sentences.zh: 楊雄衛尉箴曰：「設置山險，盡為防禦。

translations.literal: Yang Xiong Commandant of Guards admonition says: "Establish mountain peril—all become defense.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xiong's admonition for the capital guard says rugged hills serve as barriers.

sentences.zh: 」奧，深也。

translations.literal: " Ao means deep.
translations.idiomatic: Ao means "deep" or "recessed."

sentences.zh: 言秦地險固，為天下深奧之區域。

translations.literal: Speaking Qin territory peril firm—for realm deep recess region.
translations.idiomatic: Qin's rugged terrain forms the empire's inner stronghold.

sentences.zh: 注[五]前書音義曰：「關西為橫。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Former book sounds meanings says: "West of passes is horizontal.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Glossators call the territory west of the passes "spanning" (heng).

sentences.zh: 」被猶及也。

translations.literal: " Bei means reach.
translations.idiomatic: Bei here means "to extend over."

sentences.zh: 呂氏春秋曰：「神明通於六合。

translations.literal: Master Lü's Spring and Autumn says: "Divine brightness penetrates six directions.
translations.idiomatic: Master Lü says spiritual power pervades the six directions.

sentences.zh: 」高誘注云：「四方上下為六合。

translations.literal: " Gao You commentary says: "Four directions above below make six directions.
translations.idiomatic: Gao You defines the six directions as the four quarters plus up and down.

sentences.zh: 」周禮曰：「方千里曰王畿。

translations.literal: " Zhou Rites says: "Square thousand li called royal domain.
translations.idiomatic: The Zhou Rites defines the royal domain as a thousand li square.

sentences.zh: 」三成謂周﹑秦﹑漢並都之也。

translations.literal: " Three completions mean Zhou—Qin—Han all capitalized it.
translations.idiomatic: Three dynasties—Zhou, Qin, and Han—made it their capital.

sentences.zh: 注[六]龍興虎視，喻盛強也。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: Dragon rise tiger gaze—metaphor flourishing strength.
translations.idiomatic: Note: "Dragon rising" and "tiger glaring" image formidable power.

sentences.zh: 孔安國尚書序曰：「漢室龍興。

translations.literal: Kong Anguo Documents preface says: "Han house dragon rose.
translations.idiomatic: Kong Anguo's preface speaks of Han rising like a dragon.

sentences.zh: 」易曰：「虎視眈眈。」

translations.literal: " Changes says: "Tiger gaze stern-staring."
translations.idiomatic: The Zhouyi warns of tigers glaring intently."

sentences.zh: 注[七]寤猶曉也。

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: Wu means dawn-know.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Wu means "to awaken to" or "grasp."

sentences.zh: 協，合也。

translations.literal: Xie means combine.
translations.idiomatic: Xie means "accord with."

sentences.zh: 高祖至霸上，五星聚於東井。

translations.literal: Emperor Gaozu reached Bashang—five planets gathered at Eastern Well.
translations.idiomatic: When Gaozu reached Bashang, five planets clustered in the Eastern Well mansion.

sentences.zh: 又河圖曰：「帝劉季，日角戴勝，斗匈龍股，長七尺八寸。

translations.literal: Again River Chart says: "Emperor Liu Ji—sun horn wears victory—Dipper chest dragon thigh—length seven chi eight cun.
translations.idiomatic: The River Chart describes Liu Bang's physiognomy—sun-shaped forehead, Dipper-shaped torso, dragon thighs, seven chi eight cun tall.

sentences.zh: 昌光出軫，五星聚井，期之興，天授圖，地出道，予張兵鈐劉季起。

translations.literal: Radiant light emerges Zhen—five planets gather Well—await its rise—Heaven grants chart—Earth produces way—grant Zhang army tally Liu Ji rises.
translations.idiomatic: Omens of light from Zhen, five stars in the Well, Heaven granting charts and Earth revealing paths portend Liu Bang's rise.

sentences.zh: 」東井，秦之分野，明漢當代秦都關中。

translations.literal: " Eastern Well—Qin's field division—clarifies Han ought replace Qin capital Guanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: Eastern Well was Qin's celestial correlate—signaling Han would replace Qin in Guanzhong.

sentences.zh: 注[八]奉春君，婁敬也。

translations.literal: Commentary [eight]: Lord of Fengchun—Lou Jing.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Lord Fengchun was Lou Jing.

sentences.zh: 春者，四時之始。

translations.literal: Spring—four seasons' beginning.
translations.idiomatic: Spring opens the four seasons.

sentences.zh: 婁敬亦始建遷都之策，故以號焉。

translations.literal: Lou Jing also first established shift-capital plans—therefore by title thus.
translations.idiomatic: Lou Jing first proposed moving the capital—hence the honorific.

sentences.zh: 留侯，張良也。

translations.literal: Marquis of Liu—Zhang Liang.
translations.idiomatic: The Marquis of Liu was Zhang Liang.

sentences.zh: 蒼頡篇曰：「演者引也。」

translations.literal: Cangjie Chapter says: "Elaborate means draw forth."
translations.idiomatic: The Cangjie glossary glosses yan as "to draw out" or "expand."

sentences.zh: 注[九]天謂五星聚東井也。

translations.literal: Commentary [nine]: Heaven means five planets gather Eastern Well.
translations.idiomatic: Note: "Heaven" refers to the five planets clustering in Eastern Well.

sentences.zh: 人謂婁敬等進說也。

translations.literal: People means Lou Jing et al. advance persuasions.
translations.idiomatic: "Human" counsel means Lou Jing and others advocating the move.

sentences.zh: 皇明謂高祖也。

translations.literal: August brightness means Emperor Gaozu.
translations.idiomatic: "August radiance" denotes Gaozu.

sentences.zh: 西顧謂入關也。

translations.literal: West gaze means enter passes.
translations.idiomatic: "Turn west" means crossing into Guanzhong.

sentences.zh: 詩云：「乃眷西顧。」

translations.literal: Poetry says: "Then gaze west look."
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Poetry says God's gaze turned west.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]睎，望也，音希。

translations.literal: Commentary [ten]: Xi—gaze—sound xi.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Xi means "to gaze"; pronounced like xi.

sentences.zh: 睋，視也，音蛾。

translations.literal: E—look—sound e.
translations.idiomatic: E means "to survey"; pronounced like e (moth).

sentences.zh: 秦領在今藍田東南。

translations.literal: Qin ridge—in present Lantian southeast.
translations.idiomatic: The Qinling ridge lies southeast of Lantian.

sentences.zh: 北阜即今三原縣北有高阜，東西橫□者是也。

translations.literal: North mound—namely present Sanyuan county north has high mound—east west horizontal span one is this.
translations.idiomatic: The northern mound is the long east-west ridge north of Sanyuan county.

sentences.zh: 豐水出鄠縣南山豐谷。

translations.literal: Feng water emerges Hu county south mountain Feng valley.
translations.idiomatic: The Feng River rises in Feng valley south of Hu county.

sentences.zh: 霸水出藍田谷。

translations.literal: Ba water emerges Lantian valley.
translations.idiomatic: The Ba River issues from Lantian valley.

sentences.zh: 三秦記曰：「龍首山六十里，頭入渭水，尾達樊川。

translations.literal: Record of Three Qin says: "Dragon Head mountain sixty li—head enters Wei water—tail reaches Fanchuan.
translations.idiomatic: The Record of Three Qin describes Dragon Head ridge—sixty li with head in the Wei and tail at Fanchuan.

sentences.zh: 」在傍曰挾，在上曰據也。

translations.literal: " At side called embrace—at above called occupy.
translations.idiomatic: "Flank" means beside; "occupy" means commanding from above.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]肇，始也。

translations.literal: Commentary [eleven]: Zhao means begin.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Zhao means "to begin."

sentences.zh: 始自高祖，終於平帝，為十二代也。

translations.literal: Begin from Emperor Gaozu—end at Emperor Ping—twelve generations.
translations.idiomatic: From Gaozu through Emperor Ping spans twelve reigns.

sentences.zh: 注[一二]金城言堅固也。

translations.literal: Commentary [twelve]: Metal walls speaks firm solid.
translations.idiomatic: Note: "Metal walls" stresses impregnability.

sentences.zh: 張良曰：「金城千里。

translations.literal: Zhang Liang said: "Metal walls thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang spoke of ramparts stretching a thousand li.

sentences.zh: 」杜預注左傳云：「方丈為堵，三堵為雉。
